name: build
context: Build Docker images from Dockerfiles with multi-stage builds and layer caching
info: This skill covers writing Dockerfiles, building images with `docker build` or `docker buildx`, leveraging multi-stage builds for smaller production images, and optimizing layer caching for fast rebuilds. BuildKit is the modern build backend that enables parallel stage execution, secret mounts, SSH forwarding, and advanced caching strategies. Build arguments parameterize builds at build time. The target flag selects a specific stage in a multi-stage Dockerfile. Image tagging follows the `repository:tag` convention. Best practices include ordering layers from least to most frequently changed, using `.dockerignore`, and choosing minimal base images like `alpine` or `distroless`.
body:
# Building Docker Images

Building a Docker image involves writing a Dockerfile that defines the image layers, then running `docker build` to execute the instructions and produce a tagged image. Each instruction (`FROM`, `RUN`, `COPY`, etc.) creates a layer that is cached and reused on subsequent builds if the inputs haven't changed.

## Dockerfile Syntax

```dockerfile
# Base image
FROM node:20-alpine AS base

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy dependency manifests first (cache-friendly)
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Install dependencies
RUN npm ci --production

# Copy application source
COPY . .

# Expose port
EXPOSE 3000

# Start command
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

## Multi-Stage Builds

Multi-stage builds use multiple `FROM` statements to separate build-time dependencies from the runtime image. This drastically reduces final image size.

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Production
FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

Use `--target builder` to build only a specific stage. See [[docker/compose#services]] for how Compose leverages build targets.

## Build Arguments

Build arguments parameterize the build without hardcoding values:

```dockerfile
ARG NODE_VERSION=20
FROM node:${NODE_VERSION}-alpine

ARG APP_ENV=production
ENV APP_ENV=${APP_ENV}
```

```bash
docker build --build-arg NODE_VERSION=18 --build-arg APP_ENV=staging -t myapp:staging .
```

## Layer Caching

Docker caches each layer. If an instruction and its inputs haven't changed, the cached layer is reused. Optimize by:

1. **Order layers from least to most frequently changed** — base image and system packages first, application code last
2. **Copy dependency manifests before source** — `COPY package.json` before `COPY .` so dependency install is cached when only source changes
3. **Use `.dockerignore`** — exclude `node_modules`, `.git`, build artifacts to prevent unnecessary cache invalidation
4. **Leverage BuildKit cache mounts** — `RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm ci` persists the npm cache across builds

## BuildKit Features

Enable BuildKit with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1` or use `docker buildx`:

- **Parallel execution** — independent stages build concurrently
- **Cache mounts** — persist package manager caches across builds
- **Secret mounts** — `RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mytoken` exposes secrets without baking them into layers
- **SSH forwarding** — `RUN --mount=type=ssh git clone` for private repos

## Best Practices

- Use minimal base images: `alpine`, `slim`, or `distroless`
- Run as non-root user: `USER node` or `USER 1000`
- Pin versions: `FROM node:20.11.1-alpine` not `FROM node:latest`
- Combine `RUN` commands to reduce layers: `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`
- Use `HEALTHCHECK` for container health monitoring (see [[docker/compose#healthchecks]])
- Tag images for [[docker/registry]] with the full repository path

sections.title: Dockerfile syntax
sections.body: A Dockerfile is a text file with instructions: `FROM` sets the base image, `WORKDIR` sets the working directory, `COPY` and `ADD` bring files into the image, `RUN` executes commands, `EXPOSE` documents ports, `ENV` sets environment variables, `ARG` defines build-time variables, `CMD` and `ENTRYPOINT` define the default command. Each instruction creates a cached layer.

sections.title: Multi-stage builds
sections.body: Use multiple `FROM` statements to separate build and runtime stages. `COPY --from=builder` copies artifacts from a named stage. The final stage contains only runtime dependencies, reducing image size by 50-90%. Use `--target` to build a specific stage. Stages run in parallel with BuildKit when they don't depend on each other.

sections.title: Build arguments
sections.body: Define with `ARG name=default` in the Dockerfile, pass with `--build-arg name=value` at build time. Build args are available during the build but not at runtime (use `ENV` to persist). Common uses: base image version, build environment, feature flags. Never pass secrets as build args — they are visible in image history. Use BuildKit secret mounts instead.

sections.title: Layer caching strategies
sections.body: Docker caches each layer and reuses it if the instruction and inputs are unchanged. Order instructions from least to most frequently changed. Copy dependency manifests before source code. Use `.dockerignore` to exclude files that cause unnecessary cache invalidation. BuildKit cache mounts (`--mount=type=cache`) persist package manager caches across builds. Use `--no-cache` to force a full rebuild when needed.

sections.title: Best practices
sections.body: Use minimal base images (`alpine`, `distroless`). Pin image versions for reproducibility. Run as non-root. Combine `RUN` commands to reduce layers. Use `HEALTHCHECK` for monitoring (see [[docker/compose#healthchecks]]). Scan images for vulnerabilities with `docker scout`. Keep `.dockerignore` in sync with `.gitignore`. Tag images consistently for [[docker/registry]] pushes.
